summary: Neither Astro nor code-server resumed after idle sleep. A fresh container started from the baked image; ordinary filesystem and shell state were lost. Both services relaunched successfully, so S-004-03 may proceed only with durable workspace restore and idempotent supervision.

lifecycle.wranglerLifecycleSignal: Activity expired, signalling container to stop

shellState.interpretation: The named session wrapper could be obtained, but its exported environment was gone. Session identity is not shell-state persistence.

relaunch.note: Services can be relaunched from the baked image, but relaunch does not restore collaborative workspace or shell state.

requirements: Persist or back up the mutable workspace outside the ordinary container filesystem.
Detect a fresh container without trusting cached process IDs.
Restore or mount the workspace before declaring preview/editor ready.
Idempotently start and health-check Astro and code-server on every fresh-container wake.
Treat shell and terminal state as non-durable and tell users that terminals reconnect fresh.
Use keepAlive during an actively shared session or accept a cold restore/relaunch after inactivity.
Persist uncommitted work before disabling keepAlive, allowing idle sleep, or destroying the Sandbox.
Re-establish transient port/tunnel forwarding after a container restart when applicable.

limitations: The experiment ran through Wrangler local mode and Docker because the sovereign Cloudflare account lacks Workers Paid Containers entitlement.
Remote Cloudflare placement, production eviction timing, cold start, edge TLS, and production WebSockets were not exercised.
The prior ticket's browser-IDE and production-container acceptance gaps remain unresolved.

Local transport did not expose a placement ID, so fresh-container identity is inferred from the empty process list, closed ports, lost runtime state, restored baked source, and Wrangler's activity-expired signal.
